title: Data & System Design Skill Check
tagline: Dimensional modeling, OLTP vs OLAP, SCDs, partitioning, idempotency, and scale tradeoffs

questions.prompt: In a star schema, what sits at the center?
options: A dimension table
A fact table referencing dimension tables via foreign keys
A bridge table
A materialized view
questions.explanation: A star schema has one fact table (events / measurements) in the center, surrounded by denormalized dimension tables (who, what, when, where). The 'star' refers to the shape of the join diagram.

questions.prompt: What is the 'grain' of a fact table?
options: The compression algorithm used for storage
The level of detail each row represents (one row per ___)
The partition key
The number of measures per row
questions.explanation: Grain = what a single row means. 'One row per order line item', 'one row per session', 'one row per impression'. Declare the grain BEFORE you design the schema — mixing grains is the most common modeling bug.

questions.prompt: A Slowly Changing Dimension Type 2 (SCD2) is used to:
options: Overwrite the old value with the new one
Keep history by adding a new row with effective_from / effective_to (or is_current flag) when an attribute changes
Keep just the previous value in a separate column
Delete and re-insert the row
questions.explanation: SCD1 overwrites (no history). SCD2 inserts a new row with validity dates so historical facts can join to the value that was current at the time. SCD3 keeps only previous-value columns (limited history).

questions.prompt: Which is most characteristic of an OLAP system (compared to OLTP)?
options: Row-oriented storage, many small writes, high transaction concurrency
Columnar storage, large scans/aggregations, periodic batch loads, denormalized schemas
Single-row reads under 10ms with strict ACID
Write-heavy, normalized 3NF schemas
questions.explanation: OLTP: row-oriented, normalized, point reads/writes, transaction integrity (e.g. Postgres / MySQL). OLAP: columnar, denormalized, large scans, aggregation-friendly (e.g. Snowflake / BigQuery / Redshift).

questions.prompt: When is denormalization a defensible choice? (Select all that apply)
options: An OLAP fact table where joins to small dimensions slow down BI queries
A read-mostly analytics layer where storage is cheap and query speed matters
A high-concurrency OLTP system with strict consistency requirements
A reporting wide-table consumed by BI tools that struggle with many joins
Whenever you want to make sure data integrity is enforced by the schema
questions.explanation: Denormalization trades write/update complexity and storage for read speed and simplicity — wins in OLAP / reporting / BI layers. It loses in OLTP where many concurrent writers need integrity. It does NOT improve integrity; it weakens it.

questions.prompt: Why prefer a SURROGATE key (e.g. an auto-increment integer or UUID) over the NATURAL key for a dimension primary key?
options: Surrogate keys are always faster to join
Natural keys can change over time (renames, restructures) and may not be unique across source systems — surrogate keys decouple the warehouse from these changes and enable SCD2
Natural keys are deprecated in modern databases
Surrogate keys take less storage than any natural key
questions.explanation: Natural keys (email, SKU, employee_id) can change, get reused, or collide between sources. A surrogate key gives the warehouse a stable identity for a dimension row — essential for SCD2 (one natural key, multiple surrogate-keyed versions over time).

questions.prompt: Why partition a large fact table by date? (Select all that apply)
options: Partition pruning — date-filtered queries skip irrelevant partitions
Cheaper / faster backfills — you can rewrite a single partition
It physically deletes old data automatically
Easier retention policy — drop partitions older than N days
Smaller per-partition statistics improve the planner
It eliminates the need for indexes entirely
questions.explanation: Partitioning helps with pruning, targeted backfills, retention (DROP PARTITION is metadata-only and fast), and per-partition stats. It doesn't auto-delete data and doesn't replace indexes — though it often reduces the need for them.

questions.prompt: What does it mean for a data pipeline step to be IDEMPOTENT?
options: It always succeeds
Running it once or running it many times produces the same end state
It runs in constant time
It locks the target table while running
questions.explanation: Idempotent = re-runnable safely. Achieved via MERGE/UPSERT keyed on a natural identity, partition overwrite by date, or staging-then-swap. Critical because pipelines retry on failure — non-idempotent steps cause duplicates.

questions.prompt: A late-arriving FACT problem is:
options: A fact row whose dimension keys reference dimension members that don't exist yet in the dimension table
A fact row that arrives after the partition has been queried
A fact that is deleted before it can be read
A duplicate fact row
questions.explanation: Late-arriving facts reference dimensions you haven't loaded yet. Typical mitigations: an 'unknown' / -1 surrogate placeholder row in the dimension that the fact points to, then a reconciliation job that updates the FK once the real dimension row lands. (Distinct from late-arriving DIMENSIONS, which are about historical attribute changes.)

questions.prompt: A single shard / partition / key is receiving disproportionate load (a 'hot key'). Which mitigations are reasonable? (Select all that apply)
options: Salting the key — append a random suffix to spread writes across N sub-keys, then aggregate
Pre-aggregate at the producer so each event isn't a separate write
Move the hot tenant / key to its own dedicated shard
Just add more capacity to the existing shard
Use a CDN / read-through cache for hot-read keys
Switch the entire system to a single-node database
questions.explanation: Salting, producer-side aggregation, dedicated shard for the heavy hitter, and caching for hot reads are all standard. Vertical scaling of one shard buys time but doesn't fix the skew. Going single-node makes the problem worse.

questions.prompt: When would you prefer COLUMNAR storage (Parquet, ORC, Snowflake micro-partitions) over row-oriented storage?
options: Heavy point-lookups returning entire rows
Wide tables where queries scan only a few columns and aggregate over many rows
Write-heavy OLTP
Whenever the data has more than 5 columns
questions.explanation: Columnar shines when queries touch a few columns out of many — only those columns are read from disk, and per-column compression (run-length, dictionary) is highly effective. For full-row reads or row-level transactions, row stores win.

questions.prompt: When do you need a BRIDGE table in a dimensional model?
options: When two facts need to share a dimension
When a fact has a many-to-many relationship to a dimension (e.g. a transaction with multiple categories) — the bridge resolves the M:N
When a dimension has more than 1M rows
When you want to enforce a foreign key
questions.explanation: Bridges resolve M:N — e.g. one transaction can have many product categories, or one patient can have many diagnoses. The bridge holds (fact_key, dim_key, [optional weight]). Without it, you'd either duplicate rows (inflating measures) or pack values into arrays (hard to filter).

questions.prompt: Design a star-schema FACT table for ride-share trips. Write the CREATE TABLE statement. Include trip-level measures, dimension foreign keys, and a date partition column. Don't define the dimension tables — just the fact.
questions.model_answer:
CREATE TABLE fact_trip (
  trip_sk             BIGINT      NOT NULL,        -- surrogate PK
  trip_nk             VARCHAR(64) NOT NULL,        -- natural key from source
  rider_sk            BIGINT      NOT NULL,        -- FK -> dim_rider
  driver_sk           BIGINT      NOT NULL,        -- FK -> dim_driver
  vehicle_sk          BIGINT,                      -- FK -> dim_vehicle
  pickup_location_sk  BIGINT      NOT NULL,        -- FK -> dim_location
  dropoff_location_sk BIGINT      NOT NULL,        -- FK -> dim_location
  payment_method_sk   BIGINT,                      -- FK -> dim_payment
  request_ts          TIMESTAMP   NOT NULL,        -- degenerate dim / event time
  pickup_ts           TIMESTAMP,
  dropoff_ts          TIMESTAMP,
  -- measures
  distance_km         DECIMAL(8,3),
  duration_seconds    INT,
  fare_amount         DECIMAL(10,2),
  surge_multiplier    DECIMAL(4,2),
  tip_amount          DECIMAL(10,2),
  driver_payout       DECIMAL(10,2),
  status              VARCHAR(20),                  -- completed / cancelled / no_show
  -- partition / lineage
  trip_date           DATE NOT NULL,
  ingested_at         TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
)
PARTITION BY (trip_date);
key_points: Declares the grain implicitly: one row per trip
Surrogate FKs to dimensions (rider, driver, vehicle, location × 2, payment)
Distinguishes pickup vs dropoff location with separate FKs
Additive measures (distance, duration, fare, tip, payout) — clearly numeric
Includes event timestamps (request, pickup, dropoff)
Partition by trip_date for pruning and retention
Includes lineage column (ingested_at)
Status column to handle cancellations / no-shows without separate tables

questions.prompt: Model the many-to-many relationship between USERS and ROLES (a user can have multiple roles; a role can belong to multiple users). Describe the tables and keys you'd create.
questions.model_answer:
Three tables:

1. **user** — `user_id (PK)`, name, email (UNIQUE), created_at, …
2. **role** — `role_id (PK)`, role_name (UNIQUE), description, …
3. **user_role** (junction / associative table) — `user_id (FK→user)`, `role_id (FK→role)`, granted_at, granted_by (FK→user, nullable), PRIMARY KEY (user_id, role_id)

Key points:
- Composite PK on (user_id, role_id) prevents duplicate grants.
- Optionally add `valid_from`, `valid_to`, or `revoked_at` to support time-bounded role membership.
- Indexes on each FK column for efficient lookups in both directions.
- If roles need a hierarchy (role inherits from role), add a `parent_role_id` self-FK on the role table, or a separate role_hierarchy bridge.
- For audit, an append-only `user_role_history` table can capture grants/revocations over time.
key_points: Three tables: user, role, and a junction table
Junction table uses composite PK (user_id, role_id)
Foreign keys back to both user and role
Mentions auxiliary columns (granted_at, granted_by, revoked_at) for auditability
Considers indexing the FKs for bidirectional queries
Bonus: time-bounded membership or audit history

questions.prompt: You're designing the event ingestion path for a product that emits ~50,000 events per second (clickstream, server-side). The downstream needs near-real-time dashboards (≤1 min lag) AND historical analysis over years of data. Sketch the architecture in 4-6 layers.
questions.model_answer:
1. **Producers** — SDKs / server emit events with a schema (use Avro/Protobuf for typed payloads, not raw JSON). Include event_id (idempotency), event_ts, ingest_ts.
2. **Ingestion bus** — Kafka (or Kinesis / Pub/Sub). Sized to absorb spikes; retention 7–30 days. Partition by a key that spreads load (e.g. user_id hashed) but tolerates hot keys via salting.
3. **Stream processor** — Flink / Spark Structured Streaming / ksqlDB for windowed aggregations, enrichment, dedup-by-event_id. Emits two outputs: (a) compact aggregates to a hot store for dashboards, (b) raw enriched events to object storage.
4. **Hot serving layer** — ClickHouse / Druid / Pinot / Materialize keyed by recent time window — sub-second dashboards, ≤1 min freshness.
5. **Cold / warehouse layer** — Parquet on S3/GCS, partitioned by event_date and ingestion hour, registered as an Iceberg / Delta table. Queried by Snowflake / BigQuery / Trino for ad-hoc and historical analysis.
6. **Schema registry + DLQ** — schema registry to enforce contracts; bad-event dead-letter queue to avoid blocking the main pipeline.
7. **Observability** — lag metrics (consumer lag, end-to-end latency), per-event-type volume, error budget on dropped/late events.

Key tradeoffs: lambda-style (hot + cold) gives both speed and depth at the cost of two code paths. A kappa architecture (one stream pipeline replayed for backfills) is simpler but harder to backfill cold history.
key_points: Decouples producers from consumers via a durable bus (Kafka/Kinesis/PubSub)
Separates HOT (real-time dashboards) from COLD (warehouse / historical) paths
Stream processor for windowed aggregates, dedup, enrichment
Uses object storage (Parquet/Iceberg/Delta) for the cold tier — open format, columnar, partitioned
Mentions schema enforcement (Avro/Proto + schema registry)
Mentions idempotency / dedup (event_id)
Mentions partitioning strategy in Kafka and in the warehouse
Mentions observability (lag, latency, DLQ)
Discusses a tradeoff (lambda vs kappa, or HOT vs COLD cost)

questions.prompt: A **snowflake schema** differs from a star schema in that:
options: It uses a snowflake-shaped data warehouse appliance
Dimensions are normalized into multiple related tables (e.g. product → category → department)
It eliminates the fact table entirely
It is exclusively used for streaming workloads
questions.explanation: Snowflake schemas normalize dimensions across multiple tables (product joins to category joins to department). They save storage and ease updates of repeated attributes but require more joins per query — usually a worse tradeoff than star schemas in modern columnar warehouses where storage is cheap and joins are expensive.

questions.prompt: A **galaxy schema** (a.k.a. fact constellation) means:
options: Multiple fact tables that share one or more conformed dimensions
A single fact table with hundreds of measures
A graph database schema
A schema deployed across multiple regions
questions.explanation: A galaxy/constellation schema has several fact tables (e.g. fact_order, fact_shipment, fact_return) that share conformed dimensions like dim_customer and dim_product. Conformance is what makes cross-process analysis possible — same customer key means the same thing in every fact.

questions.prompt: What is a **conformed dimension**?
options: A dimension that fits within a single partition
A dimension used identically (same keys, same attribute meanings) across multiple fact tables / business processes
A dimension that has been normalized into 3NF
A dimension that conforms to GDPR rules
questions.explanation: Conformed dimensions are the backbone of an enterprise data warehouse: dim_date, dim_customer, dim_product mean the same thing whether you join from fact_order, fact_shipment, or fact_return. Without conformance, you can't compare or roll up across processes.

questions.prompt: A **role-playing dimension** is:
options: A dimension that takes different meanings (roles) when joined from a fact (e.g. dim_date joined as order_date, ship_date, return_date)
A dimension used for role-based access control
A dimension that can be queried only by certain user roles
A dimension table that has a self-referencing FK
questions.explanation: Same physical dimension, multiple logical roles. dim_date can be aliased as order_date, ship_date, due_date in a fact. Often exposed as views (vw_order_date, vw_ship_date) so BI users see clean column names without ambiguity.

questions.prompt: A **junk dimension** is:
options: A dimension flagged for deletion
A dimension that consolidates several low-cardinality flags / indicators (e.g. is_paid, channel, source) into one table to avoid bloating the fact with many small columns
A staging table for incoming data
A dimension full of incorrect data
questions.explanation: Junk dimensions roll up several low-cardinality, unrelated flags into a single dim with one row per unique combination. The fact carries one junk_sk instead of N boolean / status columns — cleaner schema, no semantic loss.

questions.prompt: A **mini-dimension** is typically used when:
options: You want a dimension with fewer than 100 rows
A large dimension has a handful of rapidly-changing attributes — split those into a mini-dim so SCD2 history doesn't explode the main dimension
You need a temporary dimension for ETL
You are running on limited storage
questions.explanation: If dim_customer is millions of rows and 'preference_segment' changes monthly, full SCD2 explodes row counts. Pull rapidly-changing attributes into a mini-dim (one row per distinct combination), reference both dims from the fact. Main dim stays small; history is captured efficiently.

questions.prompt: A **degenerate dimension** is:
options: A dimension table with no rows
A dimension attribute (typically an identifier like order_number or invoice_number) stored directly on the fact with no separate dimension table
A dimension that has been corrupted
A dimension being deprecated
questions.explanation: Degenerate dimensions live on the fact itself — order_number, transaction_id, ticket_number. They have no useful attributes to store separately, so a dim table would be pointless. They still enable grouping and drill-down on the fact.

questions.prompt: A **factless fact table** is appropriate for:
options: Tracking events / occurrences that have no numeric measure (e.g. student attendance, promotion eligibility, page view)
Tables that need to be very small
Storing dimensional attributes
Replacing star schemas with one-big-table
questions.explanation: Factless facts record the intersection of dimensions when the event itself is the measure (count). 'Did student S attend class C on date D?' — no dollar amount, no quantity, just the row's existence. Often used for coverage analysis or many-to-many event tracking.

questions.prompt: An **accumulating snapshot** fact table is best suited to model:
options: Independent atomic events that never change
Pipelines with a defined start, intermediate milestones, and end (e.g. order → shipped → delivered → returned), updated in place as milestones complete
Snapshots of a portfolio at end of day
Aggregations rolled up to month
questions.explanation: Accumulating snapshots have multiple date FKs (order_date_sk, ship_date_sk, deliver_date_sk, …) and lag/duration measures. Rows are UPDATED in place as the workflow progresses. Great for pipelines with predictable milestones; bad for unbounded processes.

questions.prompt: A **periodic snapshot** fact captures:
options: A row per atomic transaction
The state of a measurable thing at a regular cadence (e.g. account balance end-of-day, inventory at midnight)
Changes to a dimension
Schema diffs between releases
questions.explanation: Periodic snapshots are cadenced: one row per entity per period (e.g. balance per account per day). Great for trend analysis and 'state at time T' queries; doesn't capture intra-period activity unless paired with a transaction fact.

questions.prompt: Which are properties of a well-designed **transaction fact table**? (Select all that apply)
options: Grain is one atomic event (sale, click, payment)
Measures are additive across all dimensions whenever possible
Rows are frequently UPDATED in place
Rows are append-only and immutable once loaded
Includes FKs to all relevant dimensions plus event timestamp
Has a single all-encompassing column called 'data' holding JSON
questions.explanation: Transaction facts are append-only with one row per atomic event, additive measures, and FKs to dimensions. In-place updates and JSON-blob columns are anti-patterns that break columnar pruning and idempotent reloads.

questions.prompt: SCD **Type 0** means:
options: Keep the original attribute value forever — never overwrite, never version
Overwrite with the new value
Keep history with effective dates
Move old rows to an archive
questions.explanation: Type 0 = retain original (no change tracked). Useful for fixed attributes like 'date of birth' or 'original signup date' where the source value is the truth and updates should be rejected.

questions.prompt: SCD **Type 3** typically uses:
options: An effective_from / effective_to pair
A separate column for the previous value (e.g. current_tier and previous_tier)
A separate history table
Hash diffs
questions.explanation: Type 3 keeps limited history via additional columns (current_X, previous_X). It's compact and easy to query but only retains the prior value, not the full timeline. Good when business only ever cares about 'now vs immediately before'.

questions.prompt: SCD **Type 4** keeps history by:
options: Versioning rows in place via effective dates
Maintaining a separate history table while the main dim only stores the current state
Adding previous-value columns
Compressing changes into JSON
questions.explanation: Type 4 splits responsibilities: a slim main dim_customer (current state, very wide queries fast) and a history table dim_customer_history (all versions, append-only). Good when most queries want 'current' and only a few want history.

questions.prompt: SCD **Type 6** (hybrid) combines:
options: Type 0 + Type 2
Type 1, Type 2, and Type 3 — current values overwritten on a Type-2 versioned row, with a 'current' column so you can join either 'as-was' or 'as-is'
Type 4 + Type 5
Snapshotting plus partitioning
questions.explanation: Type 6 = 1+2+3. Each historical row also carries the current value of certain attributes (Type 1 overwrite of those columns on every row), letting you analyze facts under both 'value at the time' and 'current value' lenses without re-joining.

questions.prompt: You must track historical sales by sales-rep territory. The rep's territory occasionally changes. Which design choices correctly preserve 'sales credited to the rep's territory at the time of sale'? (Select all that apply)
options: Use SCD Type 2 on dim_sales_rep with effective_from/effective_to
Use SCD Type 1 (overwrite territory) on dim_sales_rep
On every fact insert, look up the rep's CURRENT surrogate key (the version valid at the sale's date)
Store the territory directly on the fact as a degenerate attribute captured at sale time
Use SCD Type 0 — territory never changes
questions.explanation: SCD2 + 'as-was' join (by effective dates) preserves historical context. Storing the territory snapshot on the fact is also valid and faster to query. SCD1 destroys the history you need. SCD0 contradicts the premise (territory does change).

questions.prompt: First Normal Form (1NF) primarily requires:
options: Every table has a primary key
Each column holds an atomic value — no repeating groups, no arrays/lists in a single cell
No transitive dependencies
Use of surrogate keys
questions.explanation: 1NF: atomic values, no multi-valued columns. (A column holding 'red,blue,green' violates 1NF.) Modern systems often relax this with array/JSON types for OLAP — but 1NF is still a useful design discipline.

questions.prompt: Second Normal Form (2NF) violations occur when:
options: A non-key column depends only on PART of a composite primary key
Two tables share a foreign key
A table has fewer than 3 columns
There is no clustered index
questions.explanation: 2NF assumes 1NF and adds: no partial dependencies on a composite key. If PK is (order_id, line_no) and 'order_date' depends only on order_id, that's a 2NF violation — order_date belongs in a parent table.

questions.prompt: Third Normal Form (3NF) eliminates:
options: Partial dependencies
Transitive dependencies — non-key columns depending on other non-key columns
Multi-valued columns
Composite keys
questions.explanation: 3NF: no transitive dependencies. If employee_id → department_id → department_name, then department_name shouldn't live on the employee table — pull it into a dim_department. 3NF is the typical normalization target for OLTP.

questions.prompt: BCNF (Boyce–Codd Normal Form) strengthens 3NF by requiring that:
options: Every determinant is a candidate key
All keys are integers
Every table has at most one foreign key
There are no NULL values
questions.explanation: BCNF: for every functional dependency X → Y, X must be a superkey. It catches edge cases 3NF misses — e.g. overlapping candidate keys with non-trivial dependencies. Rare in practice but the formal correctness target.

questions.prompt: Which are valid reasons to **denormalize** despite the redundancy costs? (Select all that apply)
options: Read paths dominate write paths (analytics, reporting)
Joins are eliminating the benefit of columnar pruning
You want to enforce referential integrity
Schema is stable enough that update anomalies are rare or handled by ETL
You're storing immutable historical snapshots where source-side updates aren't a concern
Your OLTP system has lock contention and you want to reduce it
questions.explanation: Denormalize for read-heavy analytics, when joins kill columnar performance, when ETL absorbs update complexity, and for immutable history. It does NOT enforce integrity (it weakens it) and is not the right tool for OLTP lock contention.

questions.prompt: In Data Vault, the three primary entity types are:
options: Dimensions, facts, bridges
Hubs (business keys), Links (relationships), Satellites (descriptive attributes & history)
Source, staging, mart
Bronze, silver, gold
questions.explanation: Data Vault separates concerns: Hubs hold immutable business keys (e.g. one hub_customer per unique customer key), Links capture relationships between hubs, and Satellites carry attributes + change history. Designed for auditability and source flexibility, less for direct BI consumption.

questions.prompt: Data Vault tends to beat a pure Kimball star schema when:
options: You have a single, stable source system and BI is the only consumer
You ingest from many evolving source systems and need auditable lineage with minimal rework when sources change
Storage cost is the primary constraint
You need sub-second OLTP point reads
questions.explanation: Data Vault shines in volatile, multi-source environments: source changes only touch satellites (not the integration layer), business keys live in hubs, and history is built-in. Cost: more tables, more joins, and you still usually build a Kimball mart on top for BI.

questions.prompt: The 'data swamp' anti-pattern in data lakes refers to:
options: A lake stored in a region with high latency
An unmanaged accumulation of files with no schema enforcement, no catalog, and no quality controls — leading to data that nobody trusts or can find
Data that has been deleted but is still recoverable
A lake without a backup
questions.explanation: Without a metastore, schema enforcement, lineage, and quality gates, a lake becomes a write-only graveyard. Lakehouses (Iceberg/Delta/Hudi) re-introduce table semantics — schemas, ACID, time travel — to address this.

questions.prompt: Open table formats (Iceberg, Delta, Hudi) provide which of the following over plain Parquet files? (Select all that apply)
options: ACID transactions on object storage
Time travel / snapshot isolation
Schema evolution with backward compatibility
Hidden partitioning / partition evolution (Iceberg)
Automatic encryption at rest
Built-in SQL execution engine
questions.explanation: Open table formats add ACID, snapshots/time travel, and managed schema/partition evolution on top of Parquet. They don't supply an execution engine (Trino/Spark/etc. do) and don't manage encryption (that's the object store / KMS).

questions.prompt: **Time travel** in Iceberg/Delta is implemented primarily via:
options: Periodic full snapshots stored as separate copies
Manifest/metadata files that point to a specific set of immutable data files for each version — older versions remain queryable until vacuumed
A transaction log replayed forward
Multi-version storage tiers in the object store
questions.explanation: Each commit produces a new metadata snapshot that references the set of files comprising that version. Old data files aren't deleted until VACUUM/EXPIRE_SNAPSHOTS runs, so queries can target a past version by snapshot id or timestamp.

questions.prompt: Which partitioning strategy is best for a 10TB fact table almost always filtered by event_date?
options: Hash-partition by user_id
Range-partition by event_date (daily or monthly)
List-partition by country
No partitioning — let the planner figure it out
questions.explanation: Range partition by event_date enables partition pruning for the common predicate, easy retention (drop old partitions), and targeted backfills. Hash by user_id helps point lookups by user but not date-range scans.

questions.prompt: **Partition pruning** in your query plan stops working as soon as:
options: You apply a function to the partition column (e.g. `WHERE DATE(event_ts) = '2025-01-01'`) or compare it to a non-literal expression the optimizer can't fold
The table exceeds 1B rows
You use SELECT *
Statistics are stale
questions.explanation: Wrapping the partition column in a function (UPPER, DATE_TRUNC, CAST in many engines) often blocks pruning because the optimizer can't map the function output back to partition bounds. Filter on the raw column or pre-compute the literal.

questions.prompt: Snowflake's **micro-partitions** differ from traditional partitions because:
options: They are user-defined per CREATE TABLE
They are automatically created (~16MB compressed), immutable, and clustered by load order — with per-column min/max metadata enabling automatic pruning
They are stored on local disk
They are limited to date columns
questions.explanation: Snowflake auto-partitions on ingest. Users influence the layout via clustering keys (which trigger background re-clustering) rather than defining partitions directly. Pruning uses min/max metadata per micro-partition per column.

questions.prompt: **Bucketing/clustering** (e.g. Hive bucketing, BigQuery clustering, Snowflake clustering keys) is useful when:
options: You want to physically split data into separate files per bucket key only
You want to co-locate rows that share a key so that filters/joins on that key benefit from data skipping, without creating one partition per distinct value
You only ever query the whole table
Cardinality of the key is below 10
questions.explanation: Bucketing/clustering groups rows by key within a partition (or globally) so engines can skip data and bucket-join efficiently. Use it for high-cardinality columns commonly filtered/joined, where one-partition-per-value would explode the partition count.

questions.prompt: When does adding a B-tree index typically **hurt** more than help? (Select all that apply)
options: On a column with very low cardinality (e.g. boolean is_active)
On a write-heavy hot path where every INSERT must update many indexes
On a column always used in equality predicates that filter to <0.1% of rows
On a wide composite index when only the trailing columns are queried
On the primary key
questions.explanation: Low-cardinality B-trees rarely beat a scan; write-heavy tables pay index-maintenance cost on every mutation; trailing-column-only access can't use a leading-prefix B-tree. High-selectivity equality predicates and PKs are exactly where B-trees shine.

questions.prompt: A **covering index** is one that:
options: Covers every column in the table
Contains all columns needed to satisfy a query (selected + filtered + ordered) so the engine can answer from the index alone without touching the heap
Spans multiple tables
Is automatically created by the optimizer
questions.explanation: Covering indexes let the engine perform an 'index-only scan' — no heap lookup. Achieved via INCLUDE columns (Postgres/SQL Server) or by adding columns to the key. Cost: bigger index, more write amplification.

questions.prompt: For a composite index `(a, b, c)`, which query can the index serve **most efficiently**?
options: `WHERE b = ? AND c = ?`
`WHERE a = ? AND b = ?`
`WHERE c = ?`
`WHERE a IS NULL OR b IS NULL`
questions.explanation: B-tree composite indexes are useful only when the query uses a left-prefix of the key. `(a, b)` predicates use the index efficiently; predicates that skip the leading column do not.

questions.prompt: A **bitmap index** is most appropriate for:
options: High-cardinality, frequently-updated columns
Low- to medium-cardinality columns on read-mostly OLAP tables, especially when combining multiple predicates via AND/OR
Time-series ingestion
Replacing primary keys
questions.explanation: Bitmaps store one bitmap per distinct value; AND/OR across predicates becomes bitwise ops. Great for OLAP filters on categorical columns; terrible for high-cardinality or write-heavy workloads (locking and storage cost).

questions.prompt: **Predicate pushdown** in Parquet relies on:
options: Row-group level min/max statistics and (optionally) Bloom filters, allowing engines to skip row groups whose stats don't overlap the filter range
Compressing the file with the same codec as the predicate
Storing the data in row order
Custom UDFs
questions.explanation: Parquet row groups carry per-column min/max stats. Engines that read the footer can skip entire row groups when the predicate falls outside the range. Bloom filters extend this to equality predicates on high-cardinality columns.

questions.prompt: The **small-files problem** in a data lake manifests as:
options: Files smaller than 1KB are rejected by S3
Many tiny files slow queries because of per-file overhead (open, metadata, list operations) and inflate metastore/catalog cost
Files get smaller after compression
Parquet can't read files under 1MB
questions.explanation: Each file has overhead: directory listing, footer reads, planning, task startup. A million 100KB files behave far worse than a thousand 100MB files for the same data. Mitigations: compaction jobs, write-side buffering, OPTIMIZE/Z-ORDER in Delta, rewrite_data_files in Iceberg.

questions.prompt: Which encodings are commonly used inside Parquet/ORC to shrink columnar storage? (Select all that apply)
options: Dictionary encoding (replace repeated values with small integer codes)
Run-length encoding (RLE) for runs of identical values
Bit-packing for small-range integers
Delta encoding for sorted / monotonically-increasing columns
MD5 hashing of each row
Base64 encoding of each cell
questions.explanation: Columnar formats stack lightweight encodings — dictionary, RLE, bit-packing, delta — then apply a general-purpose compressor (Snappy/ZSTD). MD5 and Base64 expand data; they're not compression techniques.

questions.prompt: Compared to Parquet, **Avro** is typically preferred when:
options: You want columnar OLAP scans
You need a compact row-oriented binary format with embedded schema, well-suited to streaming / message buses and forward/backward-compatible schema evolution
You need to query the file from BigQuery without any infrastructure
You want time travel
questions.explanation: Avro is row-based, ships its schema with the data (or via registry), and has explicit evolution rules for additive changes — making it a great fit for Kafka messages, RPC, and write-heavy streaming. Parquet/ORC win for analytical scans.

questions.prompt: **Log-based CDC** (e.g. Debezium reading MySQL binlog / Postgres WAL) is generally preferred over trigger-based or timestamp-based CDC because:
options: It captures every change including deletes with low overhead on the source database, in commit order, without requiring schema changes
It is the only method allowed by GDPR
It compresses the data before sending
It eliminates the need for a target system
questions.explanation: Reading the transaction log captures every change (including deletes) in commit order with minimal source overhead and no schema changes. Trigger-based CDC adds write latency and code; timestamp-based misses deletes and is fragile around clock skew.

questions.prompt: Your CDC pipeline guarantees **at-least-once** delivery. To produce a correct, deduplicated target table you should:
options: Treat duplicates as acceptable noise
Idempotently MERGE/UPSERT keyed by (primary_key, source_log_sequence_number) and discard out-of-order older events
Use SELECT DISTINCT at query time forever
Add a UNIQUE constraint and let inserts fail
questions.explanation: At-least-once + idempotent application = effective exactly-once at the target. Key on PK; use the source LSN/binlog position to ignore stale replays. Letting INSERTs fail wastes throughput and risks dropping legitimate writes.

questions.prompt: Kafka provides which ordering guarantees? (Select all that apply)
options: Total order across all messages in a topic
Order within a single partition
Order is preserved if the producer uses the same key (which hashes to the same partition)
Order across consumer groups
Global order across topics
questions.explanation: Kafka guarantees ordering only within a partition. Same-key messages go to the same partition (default partitioner) so per-key order is preserved. Cross-partition or cross-topic ordering is the application's problem.

questions.prompt: Kafka **exactly-once semantics** for stream processing depend on:
options: Disabling retries on the producer
Idempotent producer (sequence numbers per partition) + transactions across multiple topic-partitions + read-committed consumers
A single-broker cluster
Manual deduplication in every consumer
questions.explanation: EOS combines: idempotent producer (no duplicate appends across retries), transactions (atomic writes across partitions and offset commits), and read-committed isolation on the consumer side so aborted txns aren't visible.

questions.prompt: **Event time** vs **processing time** in a streaming aggregation:
options: Event time = wall clock on the processor; processing time = timestamp in the event
Event time = timestamp embedded in the event (when it occurred); processing time = wall clock when the engine sees it. Correct aggregations of out-of-order data require event time + watermarks.
They are the same in well-designed systems
Event time only matters for batch jobs
questions.explanation: Processing-time windows are easy but wrong for out-of-order data. Event-time windows with watermarks let you produce correct aggregates while gracefully handling late arrivals (drop, side-output, or accumulate).

questions.prompt: A **session window** is best described as:
options: A fixed-length window (e.g. every 1 minute)
A window that closes after a configurable period of inactivity for a key (e.g. 30 minutes of no events from this user)
A sliding window of size N with step M
A window that depends on processing time only
questions.explanation: Session windows are gap-driven: a session for key K stays open as long as events keep arriving within the inactivity gap. Great for user-behavior analytics where 'session' is event-driven, not clock-driven.

questions.prompt: A **watermark** in a stream engine represents:
options: A throttling threshold
The engine's assertion that 'no events with event_time <= W will arrive (or only acceptably few will)' — used to decide when windows can be finalized
The maximum throughput allowed
A regulatory compliance flag
questions.explanation: Watermarks bound the latency you'll tolerate. When the watermark passes the end of a window, that window can be emitted. Events arriving with event_time < watermark are 'late' and handled by an allowed-lateness or side-output policy.

questions.prompt: **Kappa architecture** differs from Lambda primarily because:
options: It uses two separate batch and streaming pipelines that converge at a serving layer
It uses ONE pipeline (the stream) for both real-time and historical processing, with reprocessing achieved by replaying the log
It only supports SQL
It eliminates the need for storage
questions.explanation: Kappa: one codebase, one pipeline. Backfills/corrections happen by replaying retained log data. Simpler than Lambda's two-path design but only viable when log retention covers your reprocessing horizon and the stream engine can handle full replay.

questions.prompt: The **CAP theorem** says that in the presence of a network partition, a distributed system must choose between:
options: Consistency and Availability — under partition, you can't satisfy both for the same operation
Consistency and Performance
Availability and Durability
Partition tolerance and Throughput
questions.explanation: Under a partition (P is non-negotiable in real networks), a system must sacrifice either C (linearizable reads/writes) or A (every request returns a non-error response). CP systems (Spanner, etcd) reject; AP systems (Dynamo, Cassandra default) accept stale reads / divergent writes.

questions.prompt: **PACELC** extends CAP by stating that even **without** a partition, a system must trade off between:
options: Cost and Latency
Latency and Consistency (e.g. wait for quorum acknowledgement vs return locally and reconcile later)
Throughput and Durability
Encryption and Performance
questions.explanation: PACELC: if Partition, choose A or C; Else, choose L (latency) or C (consistency). Captures the everyday tradeoff — even on a healthy network, strong consistency requires coordination that adds latency.

questions.prompt: Under **READ COMMITTED** isolation, which anomaly is still possible?
options: Dirty reads
Non-repeatable reads — the same row queried twice in the same transaction can show different values if another txn commits in between
Lost updates with row-level locks
All anomalies are eliminated
questions.explanation: READ COMMITTED blocks dirty reads but not non-repeatable reads or phantom reads. REPEATABLE READ blocks non-repeatable reads (usually) but may still allow phantoms. SERIALIZABLE blocks all. Anomaly model is from the SQL standard / Berenson et al.

questions.prompt: Trade-offs of **synchronous** primary→replica replication: (Select all that apply)
options: Replicas are guaranteed up-to-date when the write returns
Write latency increases (must wait for replica acknowledgement)
A slow / down replica can block all primary writes unless quorum is configured
Replicas can serve strongly-consistent reads
It eliminates the need for backups
questions.explanation: Sync replication offers zero RPO and consistent replica reads but increases write latency and is sensitive to replica health (mitigated by quorum or async fall-back). It does not replace backups (logical errors propagate to replicas too).

questions.prompt: In **multi-leader replication**, the hardest problem is typically:
options: Storage cost
Conflict resolution when two leaders accept conflicting writes to the same row concurrently
Initial bootstrap
Read-only client routing
questions.explanation: Multi-leader buys write locality and availability but inherits the merge problem: same row, two divergent updates. Resolutions include last-write-wins (lossy), CRDTs (constrained semantics), application-level merge, or pushing the decision to the user.

questions.prompt: **Consistent hashing** is preferred over plain modulo hashing for sharding because:
options: It's faster to compute
Adding or removing a shard only remaps ~1/N of the keys instead of nearly all of them, dramatically reducing resharding cost
It guarantees perfect distribution
It eliminates hot keys
questions.explanation: Mod-N hashing remaps almost every key when N changes. Consistent hashing places nodes and keys on a ring; topology changes only move keys in adjacent arcs. Virtual nodes (vnodes) further smooth distribution but don't fix application-level hot keys.

questions.prompt: Range-based sharding by `user_id` (where user_ids are issued sequentially) typically suffers from:
options: Inability to do range scans
Write hot-spotting on the shard owning the highest range, because new users (and their early activity) cluster there
Inability to do point lookups
Excessive storage
questions.explanation: Monotonic keys + range sharding = head-of-line shard takes all the new writes. Mitigations: hash the key, use a snowflake/UUIDv7 with time-prefix shuffling, or pre-split the range and route by hashed bucket.

questions.prompt: Trade-offs of the **cache-aside** pattern (application checks cache, falls back to DB, populates cache on miss): (Select all that apply)
options: Simple to implement and reason about
Application is responsible for cache invalidation on writes — easy to introduce bugs
First-request latency on a miss includes the DB read + cache populate
Cache and DB are guaranteed to be in sync
Stampedes on a cold/expired hot key can hammer the DB
questions.explanation: Cache-aside is simple but pushes invalidation to the app and admits stampedes on hot expired keys. There is no automatic sync between cache and DB. Mitigate stampedes with request coalescing / single-flight, jittered TTLs, or proactive refresh.

questions.prompt: A **cache stampede** (a.k.a. dogpile) occurs when:
options: A cache server crashes and all clients fail open
A popular cached key expires (or is evicted) and many concurrent requests miss it simultaneously, all firing the same expensive backend query
Cache writes are slower than cache reads
Two caches replicate to each other
questions.explanation: On expiry of a hot key, every concurrent request races to recompute. Backend goes from 0 to N queries instantly. Mitigations: single-flight (one request fills, others wait), probabilistic early expiration, soft TTL + async refresh, or distributed locks.

questions.prompt: **Write-through** caching writes:
options: To the cache only; the DB is updated lazily later
To the cache AND the database synchronously, keeping them in sync at the cost of write latency
Bypassing the cache entirely
Only on read misses
questions.explanation: Write-through: write goes to both. Strong consistency for cached entries; writes are slower. Write-back/write-behind buffers writes in cache and flushes asynchronously — faster writes, risk of data loss on cache failure. Write-around skips cache on writes (good for write-once-read-rarely).

questions.prompt: The six commonly-cited **data quality dimensions** are:
options: Speed, Cost, Reliability, Security, Auditability, Lineage
Accuracy, Completeness, Consistency, Timeliness, Validity, Uniqueness
Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value, Visibility
Source, Stage, Mart, Lake, Lakehouse, Warehouse
questions.explanation: ACCTVU is the standard taxonomy: Accuracy (right value), Completeness (no missing), Consistency (cross-system agreement), Timeliness (fresh enough), Validity (conforms to schema/rules), Uniqueness (no unintended duplicates).

questions.prompt: The principal value of a **data contract** between producer and consumer is:
options: Faster query performance
Shifting schema/semantics ownership upstream — the producer commits to a stable schema, SLOs (freshness, completeness), and breaking-change processes, allowing consumers to build reliably
Reducing storage cost
Encrypting data at rest
questions.explanation: Contracts make implicit dependencies explicit. Producer owns the schema, freshness, semantics, and breaking-change policy; consumer gets stability + observability. Often enforced via CI checks on the producer side (schema linting, compatibility tests).

questions.prompt: **Column-level lineage** (vs table-level) helps you to: (Select all that apply)
options: Answer 'if column X breaks, what downstream columns are impacted?'
Run a PII audit — find every downstream column derived from a tagged source column
Speed up queries automatically
Trace which transformation step caused a specific column's value to drift
Reduce object-storage cost
questions.explanation: Column lineage answers the questions table lineage can't: per-column blast radius, PII propagation, and root-causing a single column's drift. It doesn't speed up queries or reduce storage by itself.

questions.prompt: In Master Data Management, a **golden record** is:
options: The most recently updated row
The single, reconciled representation of an entity (e.g. a customer) merged from multiple source systems via deterministic and probabilistic matching
The row owned by the highest-priority user
A row protected from deletion
questions.explanation: Golden records are the curated 'source of truth' produced by MDM: cluster duplicate records (deterministic keys + fuzzy matching on names/addresses/etc.), pick attribute survivorship rules per field, and link back to the contributing source records.

questions.prompt: An Avro/Protobuf schema change is **backward-compatible** when:
options: Old data can be read with the new schema (typical example: add an optional field with a default)
New data can be read with the old schema
It changes the type of an existing field
It removes a required field
questions.explanation: Backward = new schema reads old data. Forward = old schema reads new data. Full = both. Adding optional fields with defaults is backward-compatible. Removing required fields, renaming, or changing types is typically breaking.

questions.prompt: Which Avro changes are typically considered **breaking** under default compatibility rules? (Select all that apply)
options: Renaming a field without an alias
Changing a field's type from int to string
Adding a new field with a default value
Removing a required field with no default
Adding a new enum symbol (under strict compat)
questions.explanation: Renames without aliases, type changes, and removals of required fields break readers. Adding enum symbols breaks readers using older enum schemas under strict compatibility. Adding an optional field with a default is the canonical safe additive change.

questions.prompt: Time-series databases (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB) are preferred over a general OLAP warehouse when:
options: You need columnar OLAP for ad-hoc analytics
You ingest high-cardinality, timestamped metrics with continuous downsampling, retention by time, and recent-window dashboards as the primary workload
You don't care about time
You need ACID for OLTP
questions.explanation: TSDBs are tuned for append-mostly timestamped writes, time-bucketed queries, retention policies, and continuous aggregates / downsampling. They're not a replacement for a warehouse on ad-hoc dimensional analysis.

questions.prompt: Uber's **H3** indexing system uses:
options: Rectangular bounding boxes
Hierarchical hexagonal cells — each parent has 7 children — giving uniform neighbor distances and easy multi-resolution analysis
An R-tree
Spherical Voronoi tessellation by city
questions.explanation: Hexagons have the desirable property that all six neighbors are equidistant (unlike squares where diagonals are longer). H3 supports hierarchical zoom (parent/child) which is great for aggregation. S2 uses square cells on a sphere with similar goals.

questions.prompt: A graph database beats a relational store most clearly when:
options: You do many small OLTP point reads
Queries traverse many-hop relationships of variable depth (e.g. 'friends of friends with shared interests') where SQL would require many self-joins
You need full-text search
You store time series
questions.explanation: Graph DBs make variable-length path queries cheap (index-free adjacency). SQL can express them but pays O(joins) per hop and is hard to optimize. For mostly 1–2 hop queries, relational with good indexes is fine; for deep traversals or pattern matching, graphs win.

questions.prompt: **Training-serving skew** in ML feature stores is mitigated by:
options: Training with a slower model
Computing features with the same code/logic for both offline (training) and online (serving) paths, and joining features as-of the event time (point-in-time correctness)
Always retraining on serving data
Disabling caching on the serving path
questions.explanation: Skew comes from features being computed differently online vs offline, or from training joins leaking future information. Feature stores address both: single feature definition, dual materialization (offline lake + online KV), and point-in-time joins that respect event time.

questions.prompt: GDPR's **right to be forgotten** is hardest to implement in:
options: OLTP databases with row-level deletes
Immutable append-only systems (event logs, immutable snapshots, blockchain) where physical deletion violates the system's design assumptions
Spreadsheets
Caches with short TTLs
questions.explanation: Immutable systems can't simply DELETE. Common approaches: tokenize PII at ingest so deletion = drop the token mapping (crypto-shredding), maintain a deletion request register applied as a filter, or compact-and-rewrite affected files (expensive). Bake this in at design time.

questions.prompt: **Tokenization** differs from encryption because:
options: Tokens are reversible only via a separate token vault that maps tokens ↔ original values, with no mathematical relationship — eliminating leakage from compromised keys
Tokens are just shorter ciphertexts
Tokenization is faster than encryption
Tokens are public
questions.explanation: Encryption is mathematically reversible with the key. Tokenization replaces sensitive values with opaque references; the mapping is held in a separate, locked-down vault. Breach of the data store doesn't expose PII without also breaching the vault.

questions.prompt: **Row-level security (RLS)** is most appropriate when:
options: You need full-table encryption
Different users querying the same table should see different rows based on attributes (e.g. region, tenant_id, account_owner) without maintaining N separate tables/views
You want to enforce column types
You need to anonymize data
questions.explanation: RLS attaches a predicate to the table that runs for every query against it (`WHERE tenant_id = current_setting('app.tenant_id')`, etc.). Avoids per-tenant table sprawl and centralizes the access rule with the data, not the application.

questions.prompt: You discovered a bug in a transformation 30 days ago. You need to **backfill** the corrected output without disrupting downstream consumers. Which approaches are sound? (Select all that apply)
options: Rerun the fixed pipeline writing to a **shadow table**, validate, then atomically swap via view/rename
Use idempotent partition overwrite — re-process each affected date partition and replace it as a unit
Run UPDATE statements on individual rows in production
Dual-write to old and new tables during the cutover, compare results, then flip readers
Delete the affected rows and let consumers rebuild from upstream caches
questions.explanation: Shadow tables, idempotent partition overwrites, and dual-write+compare are standard safe backfill patterns. Ad-hoc UPDATEs are non-idempotent and hard to reason about; relying on consumer caches to rebuild state is fragile.

questions.prompt: **Storage tiering** (hot/warm/cold) typically saves money because:
options: Cold storage has lower per-GB cost but higher retrieval latency/cost, matching the access pattern of older data
Cold data is automatically compressed differently
Hot data is read for free
Cold storage is faster
questions.explanation: Older data is rarely read, so paying premium-tier rent on it is waste. S3 Standard → IA → Glacier (or equivalent) trades access latency/cost for storage cost. Tune the lifecycle policy to your real access histogram, not assumed access.

questions.prompt: Which **data SLOs** are most useful for a production warehouse mart? (Select all that apply)
options: Freshness — table updated by HH:MM N% of days
Completeness — row count within ±X% of expected
Schema stability — no unexpected column adds/drops/type changes
Job success rate — pipeline runs without retries above threshold
Number of lines of SQL in the transformation
questions.explanation: Freshness, completeness, schema stability, and job reliability are concrete, observable SLOs that map to user impact. LoC is a code-style metric, not a data quality signal.

questions.prompt: In the **medallion** (bronze / silver / gold) architecture, the **silver** layer is typically:
options: Raw, append-only ingestion of source events with no transformation
Cleaned, conformed, deduplicated data — joined to canonical entity keys but not yet shaped for a specific consumer's needs
Final consumer-facing aggregates and KPIs
Encrypted backup tier
questions.explanation: Bronze = raw landed data, Silver = cleansed/conformed integration layer (typed, deduped, validated), Gold = business-facing aggregates and marts. The layering separates concerns and makes reprocessing tractable.

questions.prompt: An **OBT (one-big-table)** model is a defensible choice over a star schema when:
options: You have many active OLTP writers

Your BI tool / users struggle with joins, queries are predictable and read-mostly, and storage cost is acceptable — flattening dimensions onto the fact eliminates joins and improves end-user experience
You need point updates to dimension attributes
You always need strict 3NF
questions.explanation: OBT trades storage and update cost for query simplicity and consistent performance — wins in many modern cloud-warehouse BI scenarios with semantic layers above. Loses when dimensions change frequently or the same dim is referenced from many facts (drift).

questions.prompt: The **EAV (Entity-Attribute-Value)** anti-pattern is characterized by:
options: Excessive normalization
Storing attributes as rows in a generic (entity_id, attribute_name, value) triple — sacrificing typing, integrity, query simplicity, and performance in exchange for arbitrary schema flexibility
Use of surrogate keys
Partitioning by date
questions.explanation: EAV pushes schema enforcement into application code, makes filtering/joining painful (every attribute needs another join), and destroys typing. Sometimes justified for sparse, truly-dynamic attributes — but usually a sign that JSON columns or proper modeling would be better.

questions.prompt: Real-time OLAP engines like ClickHouse, Druid, and Pinot share which design choice that distinguishes them from cloud warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery)?
options: They use row-oriented storage
They favor extremely fast scan-and-aggregate over recent data (often pre-aggregated, with stream ingestion and per-segment indexes), accepting limits on join flexibility and dimensional updates
They cannot ingest streaming data
They are limited to a single node
questions.explanation: These engines are built for low-latency dashboards on streaming data. They sacrifice general-purpose SQL flexibility (especially complex joins and SCD2-style mutability) for sub-second responses on recent slices. Pair them with a warehouse for ad-hoc / historical work.

questions.prompt: Design an **SCD Type 2** dimension table for `dim_customer`. Include surrogate key, natural key, tracked attributes, validity window, and a current-flag.
questions.model_answer:
CREATE TABLE dim_customer (
  customer_sk     BIGINT      NOT NULL,            -- surrogate PK
  customer_nk     VARCHAR(64) NOT NULL,            -- natural key from source
  full_name       VARCHAR(200),
  email           VARCHAR(200),
  country         VARCHAR(2),
  tier            VARCHAR(20),
  signup_date     DATE,
  effective_from  TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  effective_to    TIMESTAMP,                       -- NULL or 9999-12-31 for current row
  is_current      BOOLEAN   NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE,
  row_hash        CHAR(64),                        -- to detect attribute change
  ingested_at     TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  PRIMARY KEY (customer_sk)
);

CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ux_dim_customer_current
  ON dim_customer (customer_nk)
  WHERE is_current = TRUE;
key_points: Surrogate key (customer_sk) decouples warehouse identity from source
Natural key (customer_nk) preserved from source for joins/reconciliation
Tracked attributes (name, email, country, tier) — those that should generate a new version when changed
Validity window via effective_from / effective_to
is_current flag for fast filtering of the active row
Optional row_hash for change detection in upserts
Lineage column (ingested_at)
Bonus: partial unique index ensuring only one current row per natural key

questions.prompt: Design an **accumulating snapshot** fact for order fulfillment milestones (placed → paid → shipped → delivered → returned). One row per order, updated in place as milestones complete.
questions.model_answer:
CREATE TABLE fact_order_fulfillment (
  order_sk            BIGINT      NOT NULL,         -- surrogate PK
  order_nk            VARCHAR(64) NOT NULL,         -- natural key (order_id)
  customer_sk         BIGINT      NOT NULL,
  product_sk          BIGINT      NOT NULL,
  -- milestone date FKs (NULL until milestone occurs)
  placed_date_sk      INT         NOT NULL,
  paid_date_sk        INT,
  shipped_date_sk     INT,
  delivered_date_sk   INT,
  returned_date_sk    INT,
  -- milestone timestamps for sub-day analysis
  placed_ts           TIMESTAMP   NOT NULL,
  paid_ts             TIMESTAMP,
  shipped_ts          TIMESTAMP,
  delivered_ts        TIMESTAMP,
  returned_ts         TIMESTAMP,
  -- duration measures (computed, NULL until both endpoints set)
  hours_to_pay        DECIMAL(10,2),
  hours_to_ship       DECIMAL(10,2),
  hours_to_deliver    DECIMAL(10,2),
  -- additive measures
  order_amount        DECIMAL(12,2),
  refunded_amount     DECIMAL(12,2),
  current_status      VARCHAR(20),                   -- placed / paid / shipped / delivered / returned
  updated_at          TIMESTAMP   NOT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (order_sk)
);
key_points: Grain is one row per order (not per milestone)
Multiple date FKs play different roles (placed, paid, shipped, delivered, returned)
NULL milestone dates/timestamps until the milestone occurs
Pre-computed lag measures (hours_to_pay, hours_to_ship, hours_to_deliver) for fast queries
Current_status reflects the latest milestone reached
Row is UPDATED in place as milestones complete (not append-only)
Surrogate key + natural key allow idempotent upserts

questions.prompt: Design a **bridge table** that resolves the M:N relationship between `fact_clinical_visit` and `dim_diagnosis` (a visit can record multiple diagnoses; a diagnosis applies to many visits). Include an optional weight column so a visit's measures can be apportioned across its diagnoses.
questions.model_answer:
CREATE TABLE bridge_visit_diagnosis (
  visit_sk         BIGINT       NOT NULL,           -- FK -> fact_clinical_visit
  diagnosis_sk     BIGINT       NOT NULL,           -- FK -> dim_diagnosis
  diagnosis_rank   SMALLINT     NOT NULL,           -- 1 = primary, 2 = secondary, ...
  is_primary       BOOLEAN      NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE,
  weight           DECIMAL(6,4) NOT NULL DEFAULT 1.0, -- apportionment factor (sums to 1.0 per visit)
  coded_by         VARCHAR(64),                       -- ICD coder / system
  coded_at         TIMESTAMP,
  ingested_at      TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  PRIMARY KEY (visit_sk, diagnosis_sk)
);

CREATE INDEX ix_bridge_diagnosis ON bridge_visit_diagnosis (diagnosis_sk);
key_points: Composite PK (visit_sk, diagnosis_sk) prevents duplicate pairs
Weight column lets measures be apportioned without double-counting
Rank / is_primary flag distinguishes primary vs secondary diagnoses
Indexes on both FKs support bidirectional queries (visits→diagnoses and diagnoses→visits)
Lineage columns (coded_by, coded_at, ingested_at)
Bridge resolves the M:N without inflating either fact or dimension

questions.prompt: Design a **junk dimension** that consolidates several low-cardinality order flags (channel, payment_status, is_gift, is_fraud_reviewed) for an e-commerce fact. Provide the CREATE statement plus a comment on how it is referenced.
questions.model_answer:
CREATE TABLE dim_order_junk (
  order_junk_sk        BIGINT      NOT NULL,
  channel              VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,        -- web / mobile / store / partner
  payment_status       VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,        -- pending / paid / refunded / chargeback
  is_gift              BOOLEAN     NOT NULL,
  is_fraud_reviewed    BOOLEAN     NOT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (order_junk_sk),
  UNIQUE (channel, payment_status, is_gift, is_fraud_reviewed)
);

-- fact_order carries a single FK column: order_junk_sk BIGINT NOT NULL.
-- One row per distinct combination of the flags (~4 * 4 * 2 * 2 = 64 rows max).
-- Avoids four extra columns on every fact row; queries filter / group by
-- attributes on the junk dim instead.
key_points: Single surrogate PK referenced by the fact via one FK
One row per distinct COMBINATION of the flags (Cartesian, populated lazily as new combos appear)
UNIQUE constraint on the natural combination guards against duplicates
Each attribute stays a typed column (not packed JSON)
Cardinality is small and bounded — fact only needs one FK column instead of four flags
Comment explains how the fact references it

questions.prompt: Design the three **Data Vault** core tables for a Customer entity: a hub, a satellite for descriptive attributes (with history), and a link from customer to account. Provide CREATE statements.
questions.model_answer:
-- Hub: business key + load metadata only
CREATE TABLE hub_customer (
  customer_hk        CHAR(32)     NOT NULL,        -- hash of business key
  customer_bk        VARCHAR(64)  NOT NULL,        -- business / natural key
  load_ts            TIMESTAMP    NOT NULL,
  record_source      VARCHAR(50)  NOT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (customer_hk),
  UNIQUE (customer_bk)
);

-- Satellite: descriptive attributes, append-only, versioned by load_ts
CREATE TABLE sat_customer (
  customer_hk        CHAR(32)     NOT NULL,
  load_ts            TIMESTAMP    NOT NULL,
  load_end_ts        TIMESTAMP,                    -- NULL for current version
  hash_diff          CHAR(32)     NOT NULL,        -- detect attribute changes
  full_name          VARCHAR(200),
  email              VARCHAR(200),
  country            VARCHAR(2),
  tier               VARCHAR(20),
  record_source      VARCHAR(50)  NOT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (customer_hk, load_ts),
  FOREIGN KEY (customer_hk) REFERENCES hub_customer (customer_hk)
);

-- Link: relationship between hubs (customer <-> account)
CREATE TABLE link_customer_account (
  link_hk            CHAR(32)     NOT NULL,        -- hash(customer_hk, account_hk)
  customer_hk        CHAR(32)     NOT NULL,
  account_hk         CHAR(32)     NOT NULL,
  load_ts            TIMESTAMP    NOT NULL,
  record_source      VARCHAR(50)  NOT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (link_hk),
  FOREIGN KEY (customer_hk) REFERENCES hub_customer (customer_hk)
);
key_points: Hub: hash key + business key + load metadata, no descriptive columns
Satellite: append-only history keyed by (hub_hk, load_ts), with hash_diff to detect changes
Link: hash key derived from participating hub hashes, FKs back to hubs, no attributes
Every table carries record_source and load_ts for audit
Separation makes source schema changes touch only the relevant satellite
Bonus: load_end_ts (or is_current) on satellite for fast 'current' lookups

questions.prompt: Design a **partitioned + clustered** fact table for ad impressions targeting BigQuery (or equivalent). Volume: ~500M rows/day. Common predicates: filter by event_date, advertiser_id; aggregate by campaign_id. Use the syntax most readers will recognize.
questions.model_answer:
CREATE TABLE fact_ad_impression (
  impression_id       STRING       NOT NULL,        -- natural key from edge server
  event_ts            TIMESTAMP    NOT NULL,        -- event time (from client/edge)
  event_date          DATE         NOT NULL,        -- partition column
  advertiser_id       INT64        NOT NULL,
  campaign_id         INT64        NOT NULL,
  creative_id         INT64,
  placement_id        INT64,
  user_pseudo_id      STRING,                       -- privacy-safe pseudonymous id
  country             STRING,
  device_type         STRING,
  bid_price_micros    INT64,
  win_price_micros    INT64,
  was_viewable        BOOL,
  ingested_at         TIMESTAMP
)
PARTITION BY event_date
CLUSTER BY advertiser_id, campaign_id, country
OPTIONS (
  partition_expiration_days = 400,
  description = 'One row per ad impression. Partitioned by event_date, clustered for advertiser/campaign filtering.'
);
key_points: Partition by event_date — supports retention (TTL) and date-bounded pruning
Cluster by advertiser_id (filter) then campaign_id (filter/group) — leading cluster keys matter most
Includes typed columns for measures (bid/win price in micros, was_viewable bool)
Uses pseudo-id for user, not raw PII
Adds ingested_at lineage column
Sets partition retention via partition_expiration_days
Distinguishes event_ts (full timestamp) from event_date (partition column)

questions.prompt: Design a **factless fact** table for *daily student attendance* in a school district. The table records which students attended which classes on which dates, with no numeric measure.
questions.model_answer:
CREATE TABLE fact_attendance (
  student_sk        BIGINT      NOT NULL,           -- FK -> dim_student
  class_sk          BIGINT      NOT NULL,           -- FK -> dim_class
  teacher_sk        BIGINT      NOT NULL,           -- FK -> dim_teacher
  school_sk         BIGINT      NOT NULL,           -- FK -> dim_school
  date_sk           INT         NOT NULL,           -- FK -> dim_date
  attended_flag     BOOLEAN     NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE,
  attendance_type   VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,           -- present / tardy / excused / unexcused
  recorded_at       TIMESTAMP,
  ingested_at       TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  PRIMARY KEY (student_sk, class_sk, date_sk)
)
PARTITION BY (date_sk);
key_points: Grain is one row per (student, class, date) — declared via composite PK
FKs to all relevant dimensions (student, class, teacher, school, date)
No numeric measure — the row's existence IS the fact (count of rows = attendance count)
attendance_type allows differentiation without separate tables
Partitioned by date_sk for retention and time-bounded queries
Lineage column (ingested_at)
Often paired with a complementary 'eligibility' factless fact to answer 'attended vs eligible-but-absent'

questions.prompt: Your company is migrating from a single Postgres OLTP database (~2TB, used by an application AND nightly analytics queries that lock up the system) to a modern data platform. Design the **target architecture** that separates OLTP from analytics, supports near-real-time replicas of OLTP data, and gives analysts a clean modeled warehouse. Cover ingestion, transformation, and serving layers, plus key tradeoffs.
questions.model_answer:
**Target architecture (5 layers):**

1. **Source OLTP (Postgres)** — keep as the system of record. Enable logical replication (`wal_level=logical`), publish a replication slot.
2. **CDC ingestion** — Debezium (or AWS DMS / Fivetran HVR) reads the WAL and writes change events to Kafka topics keyed by primary key, one topic per table. At-least-once delivery; events carry op (c/u/d), before/after images, source LSN, ingest_ts.
3. **Bronze landing layer** — A streaming consumer (Flink / Spark Structured Streaming / Kafka Connect S3 sink) lands raw events as **append-only** Parquet on object storage, partitioned by event_date and table name. This is the immutable replay log.
4. **Silver integration layer** — Iceberg/Delta tables built by MERGE-ing bronze events into typed, deduplicated 'current state' tables (`silver.customers`, `silver.orders`, …) keyed by PK. Idempotent on (pk, source_lsn). Optionally also publish SCD2 versions for entities where history matters. ACID + time travel let analysts query 'as-of' easily.
5. **Gold analytics layer** — dbt-driven star schema marts (`mart.fact_order`, `dim_customer`, `dim_product` …) modeled per business process, with conformed dimensions and tested freshness/uniqueness/relationships. Materialized in the warehouse (Snowflake / BigQuery / Redshift / Databricks SQL) for BI consumption.
6. **Serving** — BI tools and analyst SQL run against gold; ad-hoc historical queries against silver; operational dashboards (sub-minute) against a streaming OLAP layer (ClickHouse / Druid / Pinot) if needed.

**Key tradeoffs:**
- **Lag vs cost**: CDC replication gives seconds-to-minute lag without straining the OLTP DB. Truly real-time (<5s) usually requires Kafka → streaming OLAP rather than warehouse.
- **Schema evolution**: Source DDL changes must propagate through Debezium → bronze → silver → gold. Use a schema registry + automated compat checks + contracts with source-system owners.
- **Idempotency**: bronze is append-only; silver/gold use MERGE keyed by (pk, source_lsn) so re-running the pipeline is safe.
- **Backfill strategy**: snapshot-load existing data at cutover (Debezium initial snapshot or one-off bulk export), then switch to incremental CDC; document the boundary LSN.
- **Governance**: PII tagged at silver; row-level security / masking at gold; lineage via OpenLineage or dbt's exposures.
- **Observability**: monitor consumer lag, freshness per gold table, dbt test failures, dead-letter queue volume.
- **Cost**: warehouse storage is cheap; compute is the lever — use auto-suspend, separate warehouses for ETL vs BI, partition/cluster cold tables.
key_points: Keeps Postgres as system of record; uses CDC (Debezium / DMS) — does not push analytics load back onto OLTP
Uses object storage + open table format (Iceberg/Delta) for bronze/silver — replayable, cheap, ACID
Three logical layers: raw / integrated / business — and explains the purpose of each
Mentions idempotent MERGE keyed by primary key + source LSN
Discusses schema evolution / data contracts
Discusses backfill / initial snapshot strategy
Discusses lag tradeoffs (CDC seconds-to-minutes vs sub-second streaming OLAP)
Mentions governance (PII, row-level security, lineage) and observability (lag, freshness, DLQ)
Names a modeling layer (dbt + star schema) as the analyst contract

questions.prompt: You manage a 500-node Kafka cluster and a downstream warehouse. A single high-volume topic has developed a **hot partition** — one Kafka partition consistently has 10x the lag of its peers, blocking a critical real-time dashboard. Describe how you would diagnose the issue and the menu of remediations available, including the tradeoffs of each.
questions.model_answer:
**Diagnose first:**
1. Confirm the symptom — Kafka consumer-group lag per partition (kafka-consumer-groups.sh / Burrow / Cruise Control). Identify the offending partition.
2. Determine the cause:
   - **Producer-side key skew**: a single key (or small set) hashes to that partition. Inspect a producer sample to see the key distribution.
   - **Broker-side hot leader**: the broker hosting that partition's leader is overloaded (network/disk).
   - **Consumer-side hot processing**: messages from that key are expensive to process (large payload, heavy enrichment).
   - **Partition imbalance** across brokers: too many leaders on one broker.

**Remediations (in increasing order of disruption):**

1. **Producer-side mitigations (least disruptive):**
   - **Salt the key**: append `_0..N` suffix on writes for the hot key, multiplying its partitions. Consumer must aggregate by base key. Tradeoff: per-key ordering across salts is lost (often acceptable for aggregation; not for sequential operations).
   - **Pre-aggregate at the producer**: compact bursts of events for the hot key into one summary event. Reduces volume but adds producer-side state.
   - **Sticky / custom partitioner**: route hot keys to a dedicated partition subset, cooler keys to the rest.

2. **Broker / cluster mitigations:**
   - **Reassign partitions** (kafka-reassign-partitions or Cruise Control): move the hot partition's leader to a less-loaded broker. Doesn't fix key skew but evens out broker load.
   - **Increase partition count** for the topic and re-key going forward. Existing data stays on old partitions until aged out. Tradeoff: more partitions = more file handles, controller load, and consumer threads.

3. **Consumer-side mitigations:**
   - **Dedicated consumer for the hot partition** that subscribes by partition (not group), scaled independently with more CPU/memory.
   - **Async / parallel processing within a single partition** using key-based work queues (e.g. Confluent's parallel consumer) — preserves per-key order while parallelizing distinct keys.
   - **Drop or sample the hot key** at the consumer if business agrees (e.g. health-check spam).

4. **Architecture-level mitigations (most disruptive):**
   - **Tiered topics**: a 'firehose' topic for hot keys, a 'normal' topic for the rest. Consumer subscribes to both.
   - **Switch the hot tenant** to its own dedicated topic / cluster (multitenancy by isolation).

**Tradeoffs to weigh:**
- Salting and pre-aggregation are fast to roll out but require consumer-side logic changes and may sacrifice strict per-key ordering.
- Increasing partition count is broadly applicable but expensive to undo and affects every consumer group.
- Dedicated consumer / tiered topics buy isolation at the cost of operational complexity.
- Always validate with a load-replay before flipping production traffic; instrument lag-per-partition and per-key write rate as ongoing SLOs.
key_points: Starts with diagnosis: per-partition lag, broker load, key distribution, message processing cost
Distinguishes producer-side (key skew), broker-side (imbalance), and consumer-side (slow processing) causes
Lists key salting as a primary mitigation, including its ordering tradeoff
Mentions producer pre-aggregation / compaction
Mentions partition reassignment / Cruise Control for broker balance
Mentions increasing partition count and its caveats
Mentions parallel-consumer / dedicated-consumer patterns
Mentions architectural isolation (tiered topics, dedicated cluster) for hot tenants
Closes with monitoring/SLOs (per-partition lag, per-key write rate) so the problem is detected earlier next time

questions.prompt: An Apache Iceberg table commit produces a chain of three metadata file types. From outer-most (table-level) to inner-most (per-file), those are:
options: data file → manifest → manifest list → metadata.json
metadata.json → manifest list → manifest → data file
snapshot.json → manifest → data file (only three layers)
_delta_log JSON → checkpoint → data file
questions.explanation: Iceberg's metadata is a tree: a versioned `metadata.json` points to a snapshot, the snapshot points to a manifest list (avro), the manifest list points to one or more manifests (avro), and manifests list the actual data files with column stats. This indirection is what makes snapshot isolation, time travel, and partition evolution work without rewriting data.

questions.prompt: Iceberg's **hidden partitioning** lets you write `WHERE event_ts >= '2025-01-01'` and still benefit from `PARTITIONED BY days(event_ts)`. The mechanism is:
options: The query engine automatically rewrites the predicate to add a synthetic partition column filter
Iceberg stores the partition transform in metadata and, during planning, derives the partition value from the source column without requiring an explicit partition column in the query
Iceberg auto-creates a materialized view per partition
Iceberg renames the source column to the partition column under the hood
questions.explanation: Iceberg records the partition transform (`days(event_ts)`, `bucket(16, user_id)`, `truncate(10, name)`, etc.) in the table metadata. The planner applies that transform to the predicate to skip manifests/files. The user never has to add a derived `event_date` column or remember to filter on it — a classic Hive trap that hidden partitioning fixes.

questions.prompt: Which **partition transforms** does Iceberg support natively (i.e. with built-in pruning)? (Select all that apply)
options: `identity(col)` — partition by the raw column value
`bucket(N, col)` — hash-partition into N buckets
`truncate(W, col)` — truncate strings/integers to width W
`year(ts)` / `month(ts)` / `day(ts)` / `hour(ts)`
`regex(col, pattern)` — partition by capture groups of a regex
