CREATE INDEX ix_idempotency_expires ON idempotency_key (expires_at);

-- Daily housekeeping:
-- DELETE FROM idempotency_key WHERE expires_at < now();
key_points: Composite PK (api_path, idempotency_key) so the same key is allowed for different endpoints
request_hash lets the server detect 'same key, different body' — a 409 / 422 condition
status flag distinguishes in-flight vs completed so concurrent retries can wait/serve the cached response
Stored response_code / body / headers let the server return the exact original response on replay
expires_at + index supports TTL housekeeping; 24h-7d typical
Created/completed timestamps for audit and to compute upstream latency
Bonus: explicit advisory lock or row-level lock on the PK during the first execution to coalesce concurrent first-attempts

questions.prompt: Design a **time-bucketed metrics** table for storing per-service request counts + latency percentiles at 1-minute granularity, partitioned for retention. Optimized for last-N-minutes dashboard reads and ~30-day retention.
questions.model_answer:
CREATE TABLE metric_request (
  service_name      VARCHAR(64)  NOT NULL,
  endpoint          VARCHAR(128) NOT NULL,
  bucket_ts         TIMESTAMP    NOT NULL,                  -- floor(event_ts, '1 minute')
  request_count     BIGINT       NOT NULL,
  error_count       BIGINT       NOT NULL,
  p50_ms            DOUBLE,
  p95_ms            DOUBLE,
  p99_ms            DOUBLE,
  -- (optionally a t-digest blob for re-aggregation across buckets)
  td_digest         BYTEA,
  ingested_at       TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  PRIMARY KEY (service_name, endpoint, bucket_ts)
)
PARTITION BY RANGE (bucket_ts);

-- Daily partitions, easy retention via DROP PARTITION.
-- CREATE TABLE metric_request_20250512 PARTITION OF metric_request
--   FOR VALUES FROM ('2025-05-12') TO ('2025-05-13');
key_points: Pre-aggregated grain (1 minute per service-endpoint) keeps storage and read cost predictable
Composite PK ensures idempotent re-aggregation by bucket_ts
Stores p50/p95/p99 as final values for dashboard reads, plus a t-digest blob so percentile values can be RE-aggregated across buckets (averaging p99s is statistically wrong)
Range partitioning on bucket_ts enables fast retention drops and partition pruning for last-N-minutes queries
Daily partitions are a good default at this volume; finer for very high cardinality, coarser for low
Lineage column (ingested_at)
Bonus: a clustering or secondary index on service_name for service-scoped dashboard reads

questions.prompt: Design a **multi-tenant SaaS** table for customer support tickets. Must support: tenant isolation (row-level), per-tenant retention policies, and efficient queries scoped to a single tenant.
questions.model_answer:
CREATE TABLE support_ticket (
  tenant_id         BIGINT       NOT NULL,                  -- first column on every index for isolation
  ticket_id         BIGINT       NOT NULL,                  -- unique within tenant
  created_at        TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL,
  updated_at        TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL,
  status            VARCHAR(20)  NOT NULL,                  -- open / pending / resolved / closed
  priority          SMALLINT     NOT NULL,
  assigned_user_id  BIGINT,
  subject           VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
  body              TEXT,
  retention_until   DATE,                                    -- per-tenant policy result
  PRIMARY KEY (tenant_id, ticket_id)
)
PARTITION BY HASH (tenant_id);  -- spreads tenants across partitions to avoid one giant partition

CREATE INDEX ix_ticket_open ON support_ticket (tenant_id, status, created_at DESC)
  WHERE status IN ('open', 'pending');

-- Postgres RLS:
ALTER TABLE support_ticket ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;
CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation ON support_ticket
  USING (tenant_id = current_setting('app.tenant_id')::BIGINT);
key_points: tenant_id is the leading column in the PK and every index — every query plan starts by pruning to one tenant
Hash partitioning by tenant_id prevents an elephant tenant from monopolizing one partition while still allowing tenant-scoped queries
Partial index on (tenant_id, status, created_at) for the hot 'my open tickets' UI path
retention_until column captures the per-tenant retention deadline (driven by tenant's contracted policy)
RLS policy enforces isolation at the database, not just the application — defense in depth
Composite PK (tenant_id, ticket_id) lets ticket_id restart per tenant (vendor flexibility)
Bonus: a per-tenant retention sweeper that DELETEs (or VACUUMs to cold) rows past retention_until

questions.prompt: Design a **graph edge** table for a social graph (users follow users) in Postgres, optimized for both directions of traversal (followers of X, followees of X), bulk export to a graph DB, and idempotent upserts under heavy concurrency.
questions.model_answer:
CREATE TABLE follow_edge (
  follower_id     BIGINT       NOT NULL,                    -- the one doing the following
  followee_id     BIGINT       NOT NULL,                    -- the one being followed
  created_at      TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  source          VARCHAR(20)  NOT NULL DEFAULT 'app',      -- audit: app / import / api
  PRIMARY KEY (follower_id, followee_id),
  CHECK (follower_id <> followee_id)                         -- no self-follows
);

-- Reverse-direction lookup: followers of X.
CREATE INDEX ix_follow_by_followee
  ON follow_edge (followee_id, follower_id);

-- Idempotent upsert:
-- INSERT INTO follow_edge (follower_id, followee_id, source)
-- VALUES ($1, $2, $3)
-- ON CONFLICT (follower_id, followee_id) DO NOTHING;
key_points: Composite PK (follower_id, followee_id) covers one direction of traversal and gives natural uniqueness
Reverse index on (followee_id, follower_id) covers the other direction — both lookups are index-only
CHECK constraint forbids self-follows (a common dirty-data source)
ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING makes follow idempotent under retries / dedup
created_at + source columns capture audit metadata for compliance / abuse review
Bonus: a separate `follow_event` append-only log captures unfollows for analytics without storing them in the live edge table
Bonus: bulk export query is a simple SELECT * with offset/limit pagination by (follower_id, followee_id)

questions.prompt: Design a **changelog (CDC sink)** table that lands Debezium-style change events into a Postgres staging schema before silver-layer MERGE. Must capture before/after images, source LSN, and op type, with idempotency on replay.
questions.model_answer:
CREATE TABLE staging_orders_cdc (
  source_db          VARCHAR(64)  NOT NULL,
  source_table       VARCHAR(64)  NOT NULL,
  source_lsn         NUMERIC(20)  NOT NULL,                  -- e.g. Postgres LSN or MySQL binlog file+pos as integer
  source_txid        BIGINT,
  op                 CHAR(1)      NOT NULL,                  -- c = create, u = update, d = delete, r = read (initial snapshot)
  source_ts_ms       BIGINT       NOT NULL,                  -- event time at source
  ingest_ts          TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  primary_key        JSONB        NOT NULL,                  -- e.g. {"order_id": 1234}
  before_image       JSONB,                                  -- null for c, present for u/d
  after_image        JSONB,                                  -- null for d, present for c/u
  schema_version     INT          NOT NULL,                  -- Debezium schema id
  PRIMARY KEY (source_db, source_table, source_lsn, primary_key)
);

CREATE INDEX ix_staging_orders_cdc_pk
  ON staging_orders_cdc USING GIN (primary_key);
key_points: PK includes source_lsn so re-publish of the same change is idempotent (INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING)
Stores both before_image (pre-update / pre-delete) and after_image (post-create / post-update) — needed to compute deltas and to apply deletes downstream
op column distinguishes create / update / delete / initial-read so the silver MERGE can branch correctly
source_ts_ms separates event time from ingest_ts (lag analysis)
JSONB primary_key supports tables with composite PKs without table-specific staging schemas
schema_version captures Debezium's schema id so payload shape changes are reconcilable
GIN index on primary_key supports key-scoped lookups during reconciliation

questions.prompt: You operate a 30TB Snowflake warehouse with monthly compute spend of $200K and rising. The CFO asks for a 40% reduction in three months without breaking dashboards. Describe the **FinOps playbook** you'd execute, the order of operations, and the tradeoffs of each lever.
questions.model_answer:
**Phase 1 — Attribute (week 1–2).** You can't cut what you can't see. Stand up cost attribution: per-team virtual warehouses with query tags propagated from orchestration (`query_tag = team:growth, dag:fact_orders, env:prod`). Join Snowflake `query_history` to job metadata in a `fct_warehouse_usage` model. End state: per-team / per-dag / per-mart monthly cost rollups in a dashboard that engineers actually look at.

**Phase 2 — Quick wins (weeks 2–4).** Without changing any models:
- **AUTO_SUSPEND** to 60s on ad-hoc warehouses (10-30% saving on bursty workloads).
- **AUTO_RESUME on**, and consolidate one-off warehouses where they don't conflict.
- **Result caching** — ensure session-level result reuse is on; identify dashboards rebuilding identical results minute-after-minute and route them through a layer that respects 1–10 min result cache TTL.
- **Right-size warehouses** — a workload running on XL for 90 seconds may run on L for 110 seconds at half the credit-rate; XS for short ad-hoc.
- **Identify and kill the top-10 cost queries.** Almost always 80% of cost is 20% of queries; many are scheduled but unused, or scanning a 5TB table when a 30GB aggregate would suffice.

**Phase 3 — Modeling changes (weeks 4–8).**
- **Materialize hot aggregates** that consumers re-derive on every query: dbt incremental models or Snowflake materialized views for the top-N consumed marts. Compute the rebuild-vs-read ratio first.
- **Partition + cluster** the largest fact tables on the columns the query history shows are filtered most often — measure the pruning improvement; if it's <30%, the cluster key is wrong.
- **Migrate cold partitions** to lower retention or to a Snowflake external table backed by S3 — trades query latency for storage cost (mostly storage, since compute is the dominant line).
- **Search optimization service / clustering** on point-lookup tables only if the workload justifies it.

**Phase 4 — Governance (ongoing).**
- **Cost budgets per team** with auto-alerts on burn-rate.
- **CI checks on PRs** that estimate the cost of new dbt models against a sample to avoid PR-time regressions.
- **Quarterly mart audits** — deprecate models with zero downstream usage in 30 days.
- **Reserve discount / committed-use** for the steady-state baseline once the new floor is established (only AFTER cuts — don't lock in a high baseline).

**Tradeoffs:**
- Quick wins are low-risk but bounded — typically 15–25% saving without code changes.
- Modeling changes save the next 15–20% but require coordination with consumers; communicate via deprecation windows, not surprise breakage.
- Cold tiering trades latency for cost — sell it to consumers with concrete latency numbers, not vague 'archive' talk.
- Reservation/committed discounts are only safe AFTER the new steady state is measured; locking in early defeats the purpose.
- Don't disable auto-suspend or merge teams' warehouses if it creates contention — the savings can be undone by spillage and slowness that drives engineers to ask for bigger warehouses.

**Expected outcome.** A disciplined sequence usually finds 40–55% savings within a quarter on a warehouse that hasn't had a cost review in 12+ months. The hardest part is sustaining the new baseline: without governance, costs creep back within two quarters.
key_points: Starts with cost ATTRIBUTION (per-team warehouses, query tags, usage joined to job metadata) — 'measure first' framing
Sequences quick wins (auto-suspend, result caching, right-sizing, top-10 query elimination) before model changes
Calls out materializing hot aggregates with explicit rebuild-vs-read math, not gut feel
Mentions partition / cluster verification by measuring pruning improvement, not assumption
Discusses cold tiering and its latency tradeoff explicitly
Mentions governance / budgets / PR-time cost CI to prevent regressions
Warns against locking in reserved capacity before the new floor is established
Discusses tradeoffs of each lever (low-risk vs high-coordination, latency vs cost)
Sets a realistic outcome expectation (40–55% is achievable on a stale warehouse)
Communicates a sustaining mechanism so the savings don't regress

questions.prompt: A daily dbt run feeding the executive KPI dashboard occasionally **flips a query plan** at scale: most days `fact_sales` is joined to `dim_customer` via broadcast; once a week, post-deduplication, the dim crosses a threshold and the plan switches to sort-merge, blowing the runtime from 5 minutes to 90 minutes and missing the morning SLA. Describe how you'd diagnose, mitigate short-term, and fix root-cause.
questions.model_answer:
**Diagnose first.**
1. Confirm: pull `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` (or warehouse equivalent: Snowflake `QUERY_HISTORY` + `EXPLAIN`, BigQuery `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS_BY_PROJECT`, Spark UI for Databricks). Compare a 5-minute run plan vs a 90-minute run plan side by side. You expect the join operator to differ (BroadcastHashJoin → SortMergeJoin) along with the input estimates.
2. Identify the *exact* trigger. Is `dim_customer` actually crossing the broadcast threshold (default ~10MB Spark, configurable; auto in Snowflake), or are stats stale so the planner *thinks* it crossed? Run the EXPLAIN on a small reproduction with the actual dim sizes to confirm.
3. Check whether the dim's de-dup step has a SKEW: the runs that flip might also produce a hot key that breaks the broadcast hash join even if it had been chosen.

**Short-term mitigation (same week).**
- **Force the join strategy** via a hint: in Spark / Databricks, `BROADCAST(d)` hint; in BigQuery, write the join shape as a CTE that the planner is more likely to broadcast; in Snowflake, the optimizer typically gets this right — if it doesn't, pin the warehouse size up one notch for that step.
- **Stabilize the input size** by writing the dim to a small materialized table BEFORE the join, with explicit `OPTIMIZE` / `ANALYZE TABLE COMPUTE STATISTICS` to refresh stats. Now the planner sees deterministic size.
- **Add a CI check** on the dim's row count vs a baseline so a 30% week-over-week spike triggers an alert before the morning run.
- **Move the run earlier** to give more recovery time if the SLA is at risk, while root-cause is being fixed.

**Root-cause fix.**
The underlying issue is a brittle threshold-based plan. Three durable fixes:
1. **Make the dim's size deterministic.** If it's drifting because de-dup grouping changes, the right fix is upstream: stabilize the dedup logic so the dim doesn't accidentally retain duplicates one week and not the next. Use a hash diff and an SCD2 approach so the dim doesn't fluctuate.
2. **Architect around the threshold.** Either ensure the dim stays comfortably below the broadcast limit forever (split into sub-dims; or build a curated 'broadcastable' projection), or commit to a sort-merge plan and *tune for it* with proper partitioning of the fact by `customer_sk_bucket`. Whichever side of the threshold you choose, you choose deterministically.
3. **Adopt adaptive execution where possible.** Spark AQE (`spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true`) re-evaluates the plan at runtime based on actual shuffle sizes, often catching the flip without manual hints. It's not a silver bullet — it doesn't help if the warehouse engine doesn't expose AQE (older versions, some non-Spark engines) and it adds startup cost — but it's the modern default for this exact class of bug.

**Tradeoffs.**
- Hints are precise but brittle: someone refactors the model, the hint goes stale, and the bug returns silently. Document and lint for them.
- A 'broadcastable' projection (mini-dim of just the join keys + necessary columns) is the cleanest long-term answer if the full dim is genuinely too big.
- Forcing sort-merge requires you to ALSO repartition / bucket the fact correctly. Doing one without the other is worse than letting the planner choose.
- AQE is great but adds variance in execution time across runs; make sure your SLA dashboards plot the right metric.

**Preventive measures.**
- Pin warehouse / cluster size for the SLA-bearing DAG so day-to-day capacity variation doesn't compound the plan flip.
- Snapshot the chosen plan in CI for the top-N most expensive queries; alert when the plan signature changes.
- Treat the dim row count, dedup yield, and post-join row count as data SLOs and monitor week-over-week.
key_points: Starts with diagnosis: comparing EXPLAIN plans across 'good' and 'bad' runs
Identifies the trigger as a threshold crossing (broadcast → sort-merge) and considers stale stats as a confounder
Considers skew as a secondary trigger that could break the broadcast plan independently
Short-term mitigations: explicit join hints, materializing a stabilized dim, refreshing stats, CI alarms on size drift
Root cause: make the dim size deterministic upstream (dedup logic, SCD2 stability)
Architectural alternative: commit to one side of the threshold and tune for it (broadcastable projection OR proper partitioning for sort-merge)
Mentions adaptive execution (AQE) as a modern mitigation and notes its limits
Discusses hint brittleness and the need for documentation / lint
Adds preventive measures: pinned capacity, plan-signature CI, data SLOs on the dim
Demonstrates ownership end-to-end: triage → patch → root cause → prevention

questions.prompt: You inherit a 3-year-old data platform: nightly batch into a Redshift warehouse, hand-built Airflow DAGs, no schema registry, ad-hoc PII handling, dashboards on stale data. Leadership wants 'a modern data platform' in six months with stricter governance and 1-hour data freshness for the top-20 dashboards. Lay out the **migration strategy** — sequencing, what to leave alone, what to replace, and how you measure success.
questions.model_answer:
**Anchor: the strategy is iterative replacement, not a parallel rebuild.** Promise no big-bang migrations — they fail in this kind of environment because tribal knowledge is concentrated in the existing DAGs.

**Month 0 — Discovery (2 weeks).**
- Inventory the top-20 dashboards (the only ones with the 1-hour SLA). Trace each to upstream sources. Most will share a small core of facts and dims — that core is your migration target.
- Inventory PII columns and current handling. Tag them, even if the handling is wrong today.
- Establish an attribution / observability baseline: per-DAG run times, per-table freshness, dashboard load times. Without this you can't prove improvement.

**Month 1 — Foundations.**
- **Ingestion.** Introduce CDC for the top 5 source systems (Debezium / DMS) writing to Kafka. Keep the legacy batch ingestion for everything else — don't fight battles outside the top-20.
- **Storage.** Stand up an S3 + Iceberg lakehouse alongside Redshift. Don't migrate data yet; just create the bronze landing zone.
- **Catalog + lineage.** Adopt a catalog (DataHub / Atlan / Unity Catalog) and emit OpenLineage from Airflow. This is the spine of governance; the value compounds with every model added.
- **Schema registry.** Run a Confluent / equivalent registry. Mandate it for all NEW topics; legacy producers grandfathered.

**Month 2–3 — Migrate the core marts.**
- For each of the top-20 dashboards:
  1. Identify the underlying mart (often only 3–5 distinct ones).
  2. Rebuild the mart in dbt against the lakehouse (silver → gold).
  3. Add dbt tests (uniqueness, not-null, freshness, accepted_values) for each model.
  4. Backfill the gold table in the lakehouse to match Redshift; reconcile row counts and key aggregates.
  5. Cut the dashboard's data source over with a 2-week dual-write/dual-read overlap.
- The 1-hour SLA is achieved by CDC + streaming silver + scheduled (5-15 min cadence) gold incremental refresh — not by full streaming everywhere.

**Month 4 — Governance.**
- Centralize PII tagging in the catalog and propagate via column-level lineage.
- Implement column masking / row-level security at the gold layer for sensitive marts. Tokenize at the silver layer for the most sensitive fields.
- Stand up a DSR (data subject request) workflow that uses the tags + lineage to identify affected tables.
- Define data contracts for the top 5 source systems with their owners; back them with CI checks on the producer side.

**Month 5 — Observability + SLAs.**
- Freshness, completeness, schema-stability, job-success SLOs on every gold table with alerts.
- End-to-end p99 latency tracking from source-event to dashboard-row-visible.
- Cost attribution dashboard live; per-team budgets in place.
- A runbook + on-call rotation for the platform team — governance only works if someone is paged for SLO breaches.

**Month 6 — Sunset path.**
- Decommission migrated dashboards' Redshift dependencies. Don't shut Redshift down — it still serves the long tail of internal queries.
- Publish a deprecation timeline for the remaining Redshift marts (6–12 months out) with a clear migration template (the same one used for the top-20).
- Reserve a small budget line for ongoing governance: catalog curation, contract reviews, dbt test coverage targets.

**What to leave alone:**
- The long tail of low-priority dashboards on Redshift — they cost little and pay no SLA. They migrate opportunistically.
- Source-system schemas — don't ask source teams to refactor for the new platform; the contracts and CDC absorb the impedance.
- The existing Airflow scheduling layer where it works — add OpenLineage emission, but don't replace the orchestrator just to replace it.

**Success metrics (measured against the Month 0 baseline):**
- 1-hour freshness achieved for ≥ 18/20 top dashboards by end of Month 5.
- 90% of new gold tables have dbt tests; 95% have column-level lineage; 100% have PII tagging.
- Mean dashboard p99 load time reduced by ≥ 30%.
- Zero unhandled DSRs older than 30 days.
- Cost per gold table refresh tracked, and a 20% cost reduction on the top-10 most expensive models.

**Tradeoffs to call out explicitly:**
- The CDC path costs more in steady-state ($/month) than nightly batch but delivers the SLA leadership asked for. Frame it as 'this is what 1-hour freshness costs'.
- Two warehouses in parallel for 6 months is overhead. Cap the dual-run window per dashboard at 2–4 weeks.
- Building the catalog/governance before showing dashboard wins makes leadership nervous. Sequence so a small set of migrated dashboards is visible by end of Month 2.
key_points: Frames the migration as iterative replacement, not a big-bang rewrite
Starts with inventory + observability baseline so improvement is measurable
Sequences foundations (CDC, lakehouse, catalog, registry) before model migration
Scopes the 1-hour SLA to the top-20 dashboards rather than promising platform-wide streaming
Migrates marts in waves, each with reconciliation + dual-read overlap
Addresses governance (PII tagging, column-level lineage, RLS, DSR workflow) as a distinct phase, not afterthought
Decides explicitly what to LEAVE ALONE (long tail, source schemas, existing scheduler)
Defines concrete success metrics tied to the Month 0 baseline (numbers, not adjectives)
Calls out cost / dual-run / political tradeoffs honestly
Plans a sustaining phase (deprecation timeline, on-call, governance budget) so the platform doesn't regress in year 2

questions.prompt: A team proposes a Lambda architecture (separate batch + streaming pipelines that converge in a serving layer). The strongest argument FOR Kappa (single streaming pipeline, replays for backfill) instead, in 2026, is:
options: Streaming engines are slower than batch

Modern table formats (Iceberg/Delta) and stream-batch unification in engines (Flink, Spark Structured Streaming, Databricks DLT) make a single pipeline with replay capability achievable — eliminating the two-code-path maintenance burden of Lambda, with backfills served by replaying the log over the same operators
Kappa doesn't require any storage
Lambda was deprecated by the cloud vendors
questions.explanation: Lambda's original justification — batch was the only way to do correct large-scale processing, streaming for low-latency approximations — has eroded as streaming engines gained windowing, exactly-once, and lakehouse storage gained ACID + replay. Kappa-style pipelines now deliver both freshness and correctness from one codebase. Lambda still wins when your batch toolchain is irreplaceable or replay storage costs are prohibitive; otherwise the maintenance savings of one code path are compelling.
